fragments.content: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta and presence of Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein aggregates). Motor features (cardinal signs): resting tremor (4-6 Hz, pill-rolling), rigidity (cogwheel), bradykinesia (slowness, decrement on repetitive movements), and postural instability (late feature). Diagnosis requires bradykinesia plus at least one of tremor, rigidity, or postural instability. Non-motor features often precede motor symptoms: hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, depression, anxiety. Disease progression follows Braak staging (olfactory bulb → brainstem → midbrain → cortex). Genetic forms (10%): SNCA, LRRK2, PARKIN, PINK1 mutations. Pathophysiology involves mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, impaired protein clearance, and neuroinflammation. Differential diagnosis includes essential tremor, drug-induced parkinsonism, atypical parkinsonism (MSA, PSP, CBD).

PD treatment is symptomatic, tailored to age, symptom severity, and cognitive status. Levodopa/carbidopa is most effective for motor symptoms; start low (25/100 mg TID) and titrate. Long-term use causes motor complications: wearing-off, dyskinesias, on-off fluctuations. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine) useful in younger patients (<65) to delay levodopa but less effective; side effects include impulse control disorders, somnolence, edema. MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline, safinamide) provide mild symptomatic benefit, may reduce off-time. COMT inhibitors (entacapone, opicapone) extend levodopa effect for wearing-off. Amantadine treats dyskinesia and mild symptoms. Apomorphine (subcutaneous/inhaled) for rescue from off episodes. Advanced therapies: deep brain stimulation (DBS) of subthalamic nucleus or GPi for motor fluctuations/dyskinesia refractory to medical therapy (requires good levodopa response, no significant cognitive impairment); levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel infusion; focused ultrasound thalamotomy for tremor. Non-motor symptom management: antidepressants for depression, melatonin/clonazepam for REM sleep behavior disorder, cholinesterase inhibitors for dementia, botulinum toxin for sialorrhea.

PD diagnosis uses MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria (2015). Core: bradykinesia plus rest tremor and/or rigidity. Supportive criteria: clear benefit from dopaminergic therapy, levodopa-induced dyskinesia, rest tremor of limb, hyposmia, abnormal cardiac MIBG scintigraphy. Exclusion criteria: cerebellar signs, downward vertical gaze palsy, normal functional neuroimaging of presynaptic dopamine transporters (DAT-SPECT/PET), documented alternative cause. Probable PD requires no exclusion criteria and ≥2 supportive features. Definite PD requires pathological confirmation. DAT-SPECT shows reduced striatal dopamine transporter uptake but cannot distinguish PD from atypical parkinsonism. Red flags suggesting atypical parkinsonism: early falls, rapid progression, poor levodopa response, early autonomic failure (suggests MSA), vertical gaze palsy (PSP), cortical sensory loss (CBD), cerebellar signs. Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS/MDS-UPDRS) quantifies motor and non-motor symptoms for monitoring. Hoehn and Yahr staging describes disease progression (stage 1: unilateral; stage 3: bilateral with balance impairment; stage 5: wheelchair/bedbound).
